serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fried mantou consists of 4–6 pieces weighing approximately 150 g, commonly served alongside chilli crab or other rich Singaporean seafood dishes.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Bread, white, commercially prepared (includes soft bread crumbs), fried adaptation; cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient database for fried dough products
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for deep-fried mantou exists in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB Foodo database. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entry for white bread (FDC ID 325871) with fat content adjusted upward to reflect deep-frying absorption (approximately 15–17 g fat per 100 g), consistent with published deep-frying uptake coefficients for wheat-flour dough. Saturated fat and trans fat values reflect typical palm/vegetable oil frying medium. Input record values (energy 300 kcal, protein 5.33 g, fat 16.67 g, carbohydrate 30 g) are consistent with this adaptation and are retained. Sodium 200 mg/100 g is plausible for a lightly salted dough product.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Foods: a Retrospective Study — White bread and fried wheat-flour dough analogues; University of Sydney GI Database reference for white bread (GI 75)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for deep-fried mantou in the University of Sydney GI Database. White bread (GI 75, glucose reference) is used as the closest analogous food, as mantou is made from refined wheat flour with a similar starch matrix. Deep-frying creates a rapidly digestible starch structure, supporting a GI estimate at or above white bread. GI 75 is therefore a conservative lower-bound estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI 75 × available carbohydrate per serving 45 g) / 100 = 33.8. Available carbohydrate per serving = 30 g/100 g × 150 g serving = 45 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.67 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g threshold; sodium 200 mg/100 g at borderline level; low dietary fibre (1.33 g/100 g) and absent potassium data preclude offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit fried mantou due to its high saturated fat content (6.67 g/100 g), which contributes to arterial stiffness; request steamed mantou as a lower-fat alternative and avoid high-sodium dipping sauces.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High GI (~75), refined wheat flour, low dietary fibre (1.33 g/100 g), and deep-frying create a rapidly digestible starch matrix driving postprandial glucose spikes
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit fried mantou given its high glycemic index (~75), high glycemic load per serving (~33.8), and low fibre content; steamed whole-wheat mantou is a substantially lower-GI alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.67 g/100 g and trans fat 0.33 g/100 g from deep-frying in vegetable oil raise LDL cholesterol and increase cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid fried mantou due to its combined saturated fat (6.67 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.33 g/100 g) content, which adversely affect LDL cholesterol and endothelial function; steamed mantou eliminates the frying-derived fat burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that exacerbate joint inflammation; saturated fat 6.67 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried mantou as the frying process generates AGEs that worsen joint inflammation; steamed mantou is the recommended alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried mantou (炸馒头; Zha Mantou) are deep-fried Chinese steamed buns made from a simple refined wheat-flour dough leavened with yeast. In Singapore, they are most commonly served as an accompaniment to chilli crab, where the crispy, golden-brown exterior and soft, airy interior are used to soak up the rich, spicy-sweet crab gravy. The deep-frying process transforms the otherwise plain steamed mantou into a high-fat, high-calorie snack: each 100 g delivers approximately 300 kcal, 16.67 g fat (of which 6.67 g is saturated fat and 0.33 g is trans fat), 30 g carbohydrate, and only 1.33 g dietary fibre. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 75 (high), placing the food in the high-GI category, with a glycemic load of approximately 33.8 per standard 150 g serving. Clinically, fried mantou is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes (high GI, low fibre, rapidly digestible starch), hypertension (elevated saturated fat, borderline sodium), cardiovascular disease (saturated and trans fat burden), or rheumatoid arthritis (AGE generation from deep-frying). Steamed mantou is consistently recommended as a lower-risk alternative across all these conditions. The dish is vegetarian, vegan, and halal, and contains gluten as a primary allergen.
common_user_queries: What are fried buns served with chilli crab?
How many calories in fried buns?
Is fried mantou suitable for hypertension?
What is the GI of fried mantou?
What is the difference between steamed and fried mantou?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for deep-fried mantou exists in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB Foodo database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy white bread data with deep-frying fat absorption adjustment. Input record values are consistent with this adaptation and are retained. GI is estimated by analogy with white bread (GI 75) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for deep-fried mantou are not available in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: fried mantou is a plant-based wheat-flour product with no animal-derived ingredients; cholesterol is confirmed zero (naturally zero for a vegan wheat-flour dough product). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — wheat-flour dough contains no vitamin C; consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for white bread. The input record listed primary_cuisine as 'Malaysian' but the dish is canonically Singaporean Chinese in context (served with chilli crab, a Singaporean national dish); cuisine corrected to 'Singaporean Chinese'. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was listed under HPB Singapore but the URL does not resolve to an official HPB nutrient database page; nutrient provenance has been re-anchored to USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as the primary approved source with HPB as secondary reference. GI estimated by analogy with white bread (GI 75, University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for fried mantou is available in any approved source.
